Cronkite Sports class, a capstone class for upperclassmen majoring in journalism as well as master’s students, acts as a teaching hospital for student journalists who produce sports stories that air on Arizona PBS, its main partner, and more than 170 additional publication partners regularly publish their content.

In this capstone class, Students learn how to report and produce Spanish-language content across audio, broadcast, digital and social media, and showcase their work on Cronkite News and is distributed broadly.
Students report from D.C. and content is shared on Cronkite news channels and with media partners.

Arizona State's Cronkite News class, a capstone class for upperclassmen majoring in journalism as well as master’s students, acts as a teaching hospital for student journalists. The statehouse program is just one part of ASU’s Cronkite News class. Students take the class for credit. Students either pitch the story ideas or they are assigned story. Faculty members edit the work. Cronkite News also operates two websites that carry digital versions of its stories. One site is designated for the public to access those stories in readable format. The other is available only to partner news organizations across Arizona, which can pull content from that online menu to offer to their own readers, listeners and viewers. Partners subscribe to the daily Cronkite News digest and have unrestricted access to use Cronkite work however they choose, as long as they give the students credit. Cronkite News uses Slack with a pick-up channel that shows where its stories go. Any news organization can ask Cronkite News to join its listserv for access to the digest for free.

Structure: The Delta Digital News Service is a practicum course that is required for students doing a news emphasis. The program is facilitated by an advisor, and through each practicum course, students report news in the Mississippi Delta in Arkansas. Students assume various newsroom roles, including reporting on specific beats (such as education, local government and health) and sometimes also take on editing roles that involve more management of the service. Structure: Students complete work during capstone class and focus on sustainability issues in Indiana. Work then appears in USA Today-affiliated outlets. Collaborators include the Lafayette Journal and Courier, jconline.com; Muncie Star Press, thestarpress.com; Richmond Palladium-Item, pal-item.com; also Indiana University–Purdue University Indianapolis and Indiana University's Environmental Resilience Institute. Student stories typically have at least 2,000 page views. Students work first with instructor Adam Kuban, then send stories to USA Today regional editor Deanna Watson for review and publication. Students' reporting is completed through a capstone course taught by Kuban.

This program launched in January 2025 and offered every other year, aims to fill local news gaps, tackling community-focused, hyper-localized stories such as immigration, education and other key issues. The multimedia class, which fulfills a degree requirement, included five students in its inaugural year. Guest lecturers from the Associated Press, The New York Times, and The Tennessee Journal visit the class to talk to students. The editor of The Nashville Banner, a Belmont alum, also helps mentor students. The class is offered every other spring semester while the legislature is in session. The students – all juniors and seniors majoring in journalism – have opportunities to meet at the statehouse during class time and are encouraged to go up to Capitol Hill outside of class hours. The campus is about 10 minutes from the statehouse, and the students have to navigate downtown parking. They also attend the Tennessee Press Association Winter Conference, which features lessons in leadership around rural journalism and community issues, as well as investigative reporting. After an initial introduction to the statehouse, each student comes up with 10 pitches to inform future projects throughout the semester. They focus on evergreen stories over daily news; students produce a feature story and several other pieces. Funding includes $500 for parking at the statehouse, Tennessee Press Association winter conference fees, and potentially phone-pairing microphones for broadcast reporting. The program seeks to expand by engaging more students and would be open to collaborating with other institutions like Vanderbilt University
Notes: Next year they will not have data for 2026 as they are not continuing this year.
NAP Status Notes: Program received a new contact who stated they were not doing the class again until the spring of 2027. Belmont faculty member Annie Price wrote to me on Dec. 16: “This spring Belmont is not able to cover the TN Legislature due to faculty shortage. We can try again in Spring 2027”

Institution Name: Bloomfield College of Montclair State University
Structure: Students report for Essex News Daily, which serves as Bloomfield College's local media partner.

Institution Name: Bloomfield College of Montclair State University
Structure: The course pairs eight to 10 students with about the same number of publications that assign them stories and run them.
Students use the latest computational tools combined with robust field reporting to produce data-driven, investigative news stories for local, regional and national news organizations.

The Boston University Statehouse Program hosts up to 15 students per semester who are each assigned to work with an individual local digital news outlet in the state to cover state government and the Legislature, which is a 20-minute T-ride from campus. The 12-credit class, taught by former Massachusetts Statehouse reporter Jerry Berger, runs in the spring and fall and includes a 3- hour-per-week classroom session. The number of partner papers varies from 10-15 outlets. The class began in 2004 and is an optional capstone class for journalism students that runs in the spring and fall, but not the summer. Students use the statehouse as the base for the beat to report on public affairs-related issues. Students tend to be seniors and grad students, though some are juniors. Students are encouraged, but not required, to go to the statehouse in person.
Notes: Jerry Berger wrote to me on Feb. 2: “I think it’s safe to say it will continue in 2027. It’s been brought in under the broader Newsroom umbrella. While Brian McGrory has gone back to the Globe, temporarily we’re told, there’s a building infrastructure under Steve Greenlee.”

Structure:
Boston University’s student reporting program is a large-scale, faculty- edited, credit-based newsroom that operates entirely through coursework rather than internships or paid positions. They can earn 4-8 credits. The newsroom is integrated into multiple credit-bearing courses, including a State House reporting initiative. In 2025, 85 students produced 511 stories, all of which were published through partnerships with 35 local news outlets in and around Boston. In 2026 the program expanded to include more than 100 students per semester. 

Students conduct all reporting, but every story undergoes full faculty editing prior to publication, and no content is distributed without faculty approval. Faculty work closely with partner outlets to identify content needs, accept assignments directly from editors, and pitch story ideas, resulting in a 100% publication rate. Partners are primarily small independent outlets and local nonprofits experiencing reporting shortages. The program prohibits the use of generative AI for writing, though students may use AI tools in a limited, research-oriented manner akin to search engines. Overall, the program exemplifies a highly structured, faculty-led model that integrates intensive editorial oversight with strong, demand-driven partnerships in local journalism.
Pilot partnership with local access television to provide student reporting on local communities.

The journalism professors who teach reporting classes partner with the town's local non-profit all-digital news outlet. Students from the classes conduct off-campus public affairs reporting. The program is currently ad-hoc based, but the faculty intends for the program to be either curriculum-based or more structured. The first stages have been incentivizing off-campus reporting through assignments in programs the faculty champion teaches.

The Reporting Lab at Brandeis is a community-focused journalism initiative which consists of several interconnected programs that provide students with opportunities to report on under-covered communities, experiment with emerging storytelling formats and build their portfolios.Even after the semester ended in both of these courses, students were hired to freelance for Brookline.News and The Waltham Times, so the impact of this partnership is felt well beyond the time limits of a semester.

Students in a broadcast operations class and a digital and broadcast production class work in partnership with the Buffalo Broadcasters Association to produce a weekly local news panel discussion program that airs on Buffalo television station WNYO. They also distribute it in a print. This course combination runs in the Spring semester only

Student reporters cover the neighborhood that surrounds the university and produce multimedia content for a dedicated website, Buffalo Review West; their work provides news coverage for an area that lost its community newspaper several years ago.

Cal Poly journalism department started a collaboration with the local NPR affiliate, KCBX, in which the radio station hires one "super intern" who coodinates the public meeting/community coverage. Patrick helps identify this intern. The other students help produce the content and get some compensation, but the main intern plays the part of an assignment editor.
Notes: Patrick was on sabbatical for part of 2025, and he spent that time making connections with local news partners and exploring ways to improve the program

The Collegiate News Program is a collaboration between Chapman University journalism program and local nonprofit news service Voice of OC. Orange County has 34 cities and students work to provide coverage to "news deserts" in this region. Students publish in a series of courses beginning with basic news coverage and civic reporting. Students are assigned to cover the same cities as they move from introductory reporting into upperlevel undergraduate advanced reporting coursework, finally completing a two-course senior seminar sequence, during which they report and write more complex enterprise and investigative projects. Students work with faculty members Paterno and Jolly, as well as Santana, to report stories, which are then edited by faculty or staff.

Structure: At Clackamas Print, student journalism operates as a fully curricular, faculty-led media model embedded within 200-level news courses at a suburban community college outside Portland. Approximately 60–75 students enroll annually across two core classes, producing around 100 stories per year that are published primarily on the student media website, with one print edition released each academic term. Coverage focuses overwhelmingly on campus life due to limited staffing capacity—roughly 17 students at any given time—though occasional arts, culture, sports, and community stories are produced. Editorial oversight is centralized, with faculty editing student media work internally, while any stories placed with external outlets are edited by professional newsroom staff. Historically, students regularly pitched to local news organizations amid widespread community news closures, but opportunities have narrowed following industry consolidation, including the sale of a key regional publisher, reducing internship pathways in Clackamas County. The program has maintained a small, grant- and fundraising-supported paid internship pipeline, including placements at The Oregonian, though these internships are newsroom-directed and geographically oriented toward downtown Portland rather than the immediate suburban community. A strict no-AI policy is codified in course syllabi, reinforcing traditional reporting ethics and authorship norms within the student media environment.
Notes: They have 5 partnership but only one partner publish their nnews. If they're running in the student media, she edits them, or other students edit them. And if they're running in the local news, then those editors at the local papers are editing them.

Program Name: Clark Urban Community Journalism Class Blog

The Journalism Institute is a freestanding program at Colorado College that prioritizes hands-on reporting experience and long-form journalism projects through partnerships with local news publications and other organizations.

The course was created in 2002 and was offered twice in 2003. It was then taught in 2008, 2010, 2012, 2014, 2016, 2018, 2023. It did not run in 2025 due to low enrollment. I have been teaching it since 2010, when I started sending stories for publication. But it was officially from 2012 onwards that I systematically began submitting all the student-produced articles to the professional media with the goal of having them published.
Chicago Talks is housed at the college and releases stories produced in classes. Audience is external. A student editor is hired every year as a work-study who is overseen by a faculty.

Structure: In City Newsroom, a 15-week graduate-level multimedia course, students serve as local beat reporters covering New York City and create text, video and audio stories.

The faculty champion works with students to publish their work in the online student newspaper and partners with their local NPR station, WYPR. The faculty received a grant from the ACLS in 2020, which enabled them to work with students in creating an investigative journalistic podcast series called Good School, which was distributed nationally and took ~2 years to produce.
The faculty champion is now developing a structure for an assignment where students produce a 3-5 minute solo-commentary on a single topic they learned over the semester. These projects are then published in WYPR's "Know It All" podcast, and students receive points/credit dependong in the class.
Through the statewide Connecticut Student Journalism Collaborative (CTSJC), students submit stories that are shared across the state to fill the gaps in local coverage.

Structure: NYCity News Service covers New York City neighborhoods and produces special news reports on a variety of topics; the service feeds stories to various news partners and makes its content available for republication.

The emergence of the program is coming with the faculty member becoming a CCN champion. The program intends to work with the San Luis Obispo Tribune, the main local newspaper. Students will participate in the program and earn credit through associated classses (student newspaper and news writing classes). The faculty champion and their partner discussed providing coverage primarily of the community college. The faculty champion will edit student productions before forwarding them to their partner. These student produced stories will first go through the campus newspaper's processes.

The faculty champion does not have an ongoing partnership with their local news organization. The faculty champion is the advisor to the college newspaper. Students have conducted internship with news organizations in the New York area and the faculty advisor is seeking to build these relationships. The faculty champion is working towards recreating publication labs for a NAP.

Course-based with experiential learning and internship pathways. Students work on The Full360, a student-run digital news site. The news site originated as a blog but evolved into a multimedia journalism program under faculty leadership. Students produce content for class, develop reporting skills, and engage in real-world publishing with local news outlets. Internship & freelance pathways: Students have connected with The Bucks County Herald, leading to published work and job opportunities. They collaborate with The Bucks County Herald to publish student photos and stories. Sony provided three digital cameras to support multimedia reporting. Seeking mentorship & training partnerships for web production, SEO, and newsroom operations, interested in bringing in industry professionals to mentor students. The contents are focuses on: Campus & community news; Environmental journalism (strong focus on conservation, sustainability, and local environmental issues); Social media and video journalism Podcasting; Business & investigative reporting. Students collaborate with The Bucks County Herald to publish student photos and stories.

The Reporting Project is a community-centered journalism initiative that combines coursework, co-curricular opportunities, and paid experience to immerse students in locally-focused journalism with content shared and published in numerous platforms.

Classes and student media regularly partner with: Better Government Association (investigative nonprofit), City Bureau, Block Club Chicago, WTTW PBS Chicago, Chicago Reader, and others. More than one hundred students are involved in the partnership and their work reaches millions of viewers. Faculty teach a variety of journalism courses that allow students to create work for publication with media partners.

In this statewide collaborative, eight universities including four HBCUs are partnering together to provide community news through paid student reporting year-round: Louisiana State University and Agricultural & Mechanical College; Grambling University (HBCU), Dillard University (HBCU), Xavier University (HBCU), Southern University (HBCU), Nicholls State University, Northwestern State University and Southeastern University. Each school will have initially two to four students who will get paid $12 an hour for up to 10 hours of work a week during the fall and spring semesters. Additionally, each school will host one full-time summer internship where the student will be paid $12 an hour to write stories for their local community outlets. Reporting can include print, digital, broadcast and social media. To encourage more stories out of classrooms, students reporting for a class can earn a $100 stipend for any publishable story at any of these schools. Individual schools can decide whether students can also earn credit by participating this program.

Starting in 2025, Drake University covers legislation in Iowa through its Statehouse and Public Affairs Reporting course. The initiative is overseen by faculty member Amy McCoy, who also teaches public relations and political communications classes. For five weeks during the semester-long, three-credit public affairs reporting class, about 15 upper-level students from majors such as digital, multimedia, public relations, and strategic political communication operate as a statehouse newsroom. Exercises include full interviews and stories, as well as analyses of how they would approach stories and get contact information. They also cover open records and public meetings, and have guest speakers and lectures. The program seeks to balance classroom learning with real-world experience, with potential for future expansion and capstone opportunities, McCoy said. She works with other professors in advanced reporting classes when they touch on legislative work and want student reporting published. She edits student stories and works to place them.
Notes: The students produced about 60 stories combined, but we did not get placement outside of class - We had too much lag time in the stories for local newspaper pickup. We discussed partnerships with our newspaper association several times, but currently our outlets seem to have good coverage of the statehouse, so they weren’t reaching out for more.  I will say I think the public affairs reporting class has been critical in a handful of students landing jobs and some already doing statehouse coverage with their news organizations. But what the outlets wanted was interns or even full-time reporters, not publishing arrangements, and students wanted to be paid, so that’s where a partnership model wasn’t successful here.
NAP Status Notes: They are working on building partnerships. However, according to her email, outlets are more interested to have an intern rather publishing student stories.

Structure: Student reporters cover Durham and state politics and policy and content is emailed to a list of stakeholders/media partners and shared in a direct partnership with Indie Week.
Through the statewide Connecticut Student Journalism Collaborative (CTSJC), students submit stories that are shared across the state to fill the gaps in local coverage.
Notes: During 2025, we did not have any stories out of classes at Eastern or Quinnipiac. We have referred a student at Quinnipiac for a professional opportunity with NBC CT this year, and John Murphy at Eastern continues to be an active and effective board member for us, so the connections continue, but nothing on the metrics that you are seeking.

Structure: WEIU takes in students from Eastern Illinois University who volunteer to work at their daily live newscast at 5:30pm, which run by students and 1 staff overseer. Stories are checked by up to 3 different journalists in the School of Communications in Journalism. Students are not paid, sometimes they receive grant or talent board money at the end of the semester. Students earn credits if they take an internship or practicum through the school.
In Radio and TV Newswriting students put together an audio story, either a 6-8 minute feature or a 3-5 minute Storycorps-style piece which is shared with WEMU, the local radio station.
This program has partnerships with 50 news outlets, allowing student work to be published widely.

Program Name: Professional Development in Communication and Media Studies
Structure:
The university has a professional development course where students are required to work 30 hours in student media. In the past, the course had a formal partnership with an external outlet, the Fayetteville Observer, and is attempting to reform that relationship. Students who take this course must publish their stories in order to receive the credit hours.
The university also has courses under its Communication and Media Studies degree where students can gain extra credit for publishing stories in either the student newspaper or an external outlet.
Stories produced outside of the professional development are edited by the faculty. Otherwise, stories are edited by the students.
Students report on on-campus and local news for Fitchburg State’s student newspaper, The Point and local cable access TV station and republished in other local media outlets.

Florida A&M University coordinates a statehouse capital bureau through an advanced reporting class. About 20 students per semester cover the Florida Legislature and state government; the Capitol is less than a mile from their campus in Tallahassee. The three-credit class evolved out of a long-standing public affairs reporting course. It operates during both the spring and fall semesters. The class is a required course for the journalism majors at FAMU and until 2026 has been taught by Doug Blackburn, who spent more than 30 years as a reporter at multiple papers, including covering higher education and the Florida Legislature for the Tallahassee Democrat. Faculty member Reginald Grant has taken over the program as of 2026. The work is published on the student newspaper’s website and available to other outlets to republish.

Guided by experienced journalism faculty, students produce stories for a community news site that serves as a source of content for major local media outlets including The Miami Herald, The Palm Beach Post, The South Florida Sun-Sentinel and WLRN, an NPR affiliate.
Notes: She provided only Fall 2025 info.

Program Name: WGCU News/ Previously known as Democracy Watch
Structure: The reporting courses are focused on Southwest Florida government bodies. Students report on issues before those bodies, and how the outcomes of government actions affect citizens. Faculty are involved in all aspects of the production of content as well as teaching the courses associated with the program. The program is also taught in collaboration with WGCU News, therefore many of the Democracy Watch stories wind up on the WGCU News webpage, and some on air.
Notes:
Richard:
Things have changed a bit with our program since I last communicated with your group and people.
The Democracy Watch funding through the FGCU Journalism program ran out in 2024.
That was private money raised by the Journalism faculty.   The university did not have resources to pick up the funding once that three-year commitment ran out.
So we continued in late 2024 with funding raised by WGCU, Public Media for Southwest Florida.   We no longer call the program Democracy Watch;  instead we hire paid interns to work for WGCU News, which includes radio, TV and web.
Those paid interns (we call them News Fellows) are some of the top students in the FGCU Journalism program.    
I still teach at FGCU and get a close-up look at the reporting work of the top students.    We recruit from there.   I also work as a reporter for WGCU News.   
I would estimate we had in CY 2025 about 60 students in courses that produced work that could be considered for outside, non-campus publication.   
First here are the numbers on local, community news,  reported, written or produced by the WGCU News Fellows.  
Calendar Year 2025:  
 We had a total of six News Fellows for various semesters in this CY.  (None worked all year;   a couple of them worked spring and fall semesters.  Others worked only one semester).   
  They produced 70 stories for WGCU's website, 10 for the WGCU-TV newscast (PBS affiliate), and 10 for WGCU-FM (NPR affiliate).  
  Other students in the Journalism program produced four stories for the Florida Weekly publication (that is a private, for-profit publication not associated with the university.  However the Weekly editor is known to the faculty and loves to get some free-lance stories from our top students)! 
 Other students in Journalism classes produced about 10 stories for the WGCU website.  No pay to these students;  they agreed to publication of excellent stories to add to their published material, what used to be called the clip file.  
 As far as I know, the only outside media partners are WGCU News and Florida Weekly.   Yes some students have paid internships with local TV News departments, but those are not on air or in print.   Those jobs are mostly helping on the assignment desk, editing some network feed video, or pulling material for the station website and/or social media.
That's about it..hope this helps!

Structure: A faculty-advised, hands on program involving both new and traditional media including multi-media reporting with content shared with Miami news outlets.
A faculty-advised, hands on program producing content in Spanish, involving both new and traditional media including multi-media reporting with content shared with Miami news outlets.

The Statehouse File is the flagship immersive program of the Pulliam School of Journalism at Franklin College, located in Franklin, Indiana, 20 miles south of the capital city of Indianapolis. Covering its 20th legislative session in 2025, it offers daily Indiana politics and culture coverage from a full-time office in the Indiana Statehouse. Every Franklin multimedia journalism major completes a 12-credit internship semester at the Statehouse, but students can also earn one activity credit, take a three-credit class, or work as a freelancer for a stipend under the guidance of experienced journalist and editor Colleen Steffen. TheStatehouseFile.com operates year-round with between three and 12 student journalists.

Structure:
A complex, multi-layered system currently moving toward a "teaching hospital" model to integrate professional public media with student experiential learning to provide deeper community coverage while fulfilling the university's academic mission
.
The structural components of the IU program include:
Integrated Public Media: This division includes a professional TV station and radio station, as well as student media outlets like the Indiana Daily Student (IDS) newspaper, a low-power FM student radio station, and a student TV production group
While content is currently shared on university-hosted platforms like the IDS and Indiana Public Media websites, the program is working to build a formal syndication system
. This would allow student-produced and professionally edited content to be distributed to news organizations across the state

The Mobile and Social Journalism course is course-based, with a strong focus on practical, hands-on reporting. The program encourages students to publish their work on Ithaca Week if it meets professional editorial standards. They have a news-academic partnership with The Rochester Beacon, a nonprofit community online news site. The goal is to create a pipeline for students from Ithaca College and Rochester-area colleges to contribute to local journalism. The program emphasizes mobile and social journalism, investigative reporting, and podcasting. Students gain experience in multimedia storytelling, social media reporting, and investigative journalism. This program has a news-academic partnership with The Rochester Beacon, a nonprofit community online news site, to create a pipeline for students from Ithaca College and Rochester-area colleges to contribute to local journalism.

The work from the courses of this program are published on the college's digital platform, The Volunteer. The main emphasis has been sports, though students have also covered community topics. The stories aren't yet co-published with outside organizations, though the faculty champion has a relationship with an R1 univeristy and intends to partner with their media program.

Cliff Anthony is both the faculty adviser for the student newspaper  and also teaches a journalism course. He uses both as sources for stories that can be pitched to local news partners. The "partnerships" with local media are informal and based on a pitching model. Anthony identifies good story ideas and asks students if they are willing to cover them; once a story is underway, he pitches it to local editors at outlets like cleveland.com, The Land, and the Gazette. External Editing Process: When students write for mainstream outlets, Anthony’s involvement is "much greater" to ensure a "clean copy" for understaffed professional newsrooms. He reviews the work for content "holes," suggests new leads, and fixes AP style errors before submission. Distribution and Output: In addition to digital content, the program produces one print edition per month. The print edition includes links to stories that appeared online to drive traffic to the web.

Fresh Take Georgia is a comprehensive learning experience and real-world digital reporting initiative run by the Center for Sustainable Journalism, School of Communication and Media at Kennesaw University. Students are taught by faculty, with mentorship from multiple professional journalists with experience at Atlanta-based news organizations. Students work each semester in a variety of roles including reporting, editing, photography, audience engagement, digital production and podcast production. Students cover the Georgia General Assembly during the spring legislative session as well as enterprise topics of interest for a statewide audience.

Program Name: LSU Cold Case Project (Agricultural & Mechanical College)
Structure:
Louisiana State University’s Cold Case Project is a senior level class within the Manship School of Mass Communication. Through the course, taught by Christopher Drew, students investigate Civil Rights-era cold cases. The school started the Cold Case Project in 2009 to help a weekly newspaper editor obtain old FBI files on Ku Klux Klan murders. Since then, the students have obtained 175,000 pages of FBI files on killings by the Klan and by law enforcement officers. The students began producing multimedia narrative series after Drew was notified by the FBI and the National Archives in 2018 that three subject files related to Civil Rights-era investigations, totaling more than 2,000 pages, were ready. The files led to several series that won regional awards. Each semester, Drew selects specific cases or themes, which student journalists probe through public records, interviews and other techniques. The class meets once a week for an hour and a half, giving students time to work on aspects of the investigation outside of class hours. Enrollment is typically between 10-13 students. Students’ reporting on the shooting deaths of two Southern University students in 1972 by a sheriff’s deputy won a national investigative reporting award from Investigative Reporters & Editors in 2022.

Distribution: Students’ work is made available for any news publication to pick up free of charge. Drew notifies outlets about the stories through an email list maintained for distributing LSU’s Statehouse Bureau stories. Students have also produced digital products, like videos and two podcast series, which were distributed through local public radio stations.

Program Name: Manship School Statehouse Bureau (Agricultural & Mechanical College)
Structure:
The Louisiana State University Manship School Statehouse Bureau has been covering the statehouse in Baton Rouge since 2016. The program includes a prerequisite class in the fall, a spring class based at the Capitol, which is just a short drive from campus during the legislative session, and a paid summer program for a handful of students to cover the end of the session once the semester ends. The program is managed by faculty member Christopher Drew, who had a 40-year career as a journalist prior to starting at LSU in 2017. There are typically 10 to 12 students a semester. Students receive 3 credits and can take the class twice. Students are required to spend at least one day in the legislature.

Distribution: 4 publications, digital publications, newspapers, and radio/TV have run at least one of their stories and many take content on a regular basis.

Program Name: Louisiana Collegiate News Collaborative (Agricultural & Mechanical College)
Structure:
The Louisiana Collegiate News Collaborative appears to be the first of its kind in the U.S. Louisiana State University, which is leading the collaborative, is working with seven other higher education institutions in Louisiana to work with faculty and news outlets to produce student-reported community news across the state. Those schools are, Grambling University (HBCU), Dillard University (HBCU), Xavier University (HBCU), Southern University (HBCU), Nicholls State University, Northwestern State University and Southeastern Louisiana University. The collaborative is headed by two faculty at LSU, student media advisor Jeff Gauger and professional-in-residence Lisa Page, who graduated from Dillard University before working at the Times-Picayune and Washington Post. Gauger sends out a weekly email to the network of participating faculty and editors. He is also forming a website for the collaboration. Each school will have initially TO Most of the schools have two to four students who (will get/are) paid $12 an hour for up to 10 hours of work a week during the fall and spring semesters. Additionally, (each school will/most of the schools host one full-time summer internship where the student will be paid $12 an hour to write stories for their local community outlets. To encourage more stories out of classrooms, students reporting for a class can earn a $100 stipend for any publishable story at any of these schools. Individual schools can decide whether students can also earn credit by participating this program.

Distribution: Reporting can include print, digital, broadcast and social media.

The program is part of the university's Department of Mass Communication. Students previously published outside of the program with local outlets, the station has brought in professionals from external outlets. The program is a special topics class for 3 hours of credit per semester. Stories are edited every Monday & Friday by the faculty champion.
Community reporting takes place in a course related to the Greyhound student newspaper and is shared with the Baltimore Fishbowl local news platform.
Students in a documentary production class at Marist University partner with local nonprofit newsrooms to create short video documentaries.
Students produce journalistic work for publication with media partners and the university website.

Students collaborate with the online news publication Bucket List Community Cafe in Denver alongside  CU Boulder and University of Denver students, and all students must produce six articles per semester.

Students write for the university created news outlet that covers the greater community and produces about twelve stories and eight briefs per week under the guidance of a faculty editor. The program does not have collaborators as it operates as an in-house media outlet. The website produces about 12 stories and 8 briefs per week. The editor of the Oxford Observer pushes the paper out to readers through social media and through email. Two faculty members are funded and supported by the university to lead the courses available to students, functioning as instructors as well as editors. Additionally, the university funds the upkeep of the website the Observer is hosted on. During breaks, faculty are paid through a grant from the Menard Family Center for Democracy. Funding comes from Miami University (upkeep of website, faculty who teach courses), Oxford Community Foundation ($50,000), and the Menard Family Center for Democracy (pays faculty members during breaks). A required multimedia journalism course produces content for the Oxford Observer, a news site run by the university, covering local issues.

The Food Fix was launched as a collaboration with Michigan State University’s Global Center of Food Systems Innovation, through which researchers work on food issues in developing countries. Initially serving as a forum to help scientists and researchers better tell the stories of their research, the program began hiring journalism students to write about food systems innovations. The Food Fix includes a mixture of a podcast and written articles. The podcast launched when a plant science graduate student pitched the idea. Through the podcast, researchers and student journalists are interviewed about various food systems projects happening around the world and in Michigan. The podcast is hosted by a paid student host, often a graduate student. The program grew out of a partnership with the MSU Global Center for Food Systems Innovation. Poulson says that partnering with a non-journalistic center opened up a range of opportunities for tapping into expertise, student participation, and financial resources.

The Food Fix website also publishes articles by students enrolled in MSU’s various environmental journalism classes, when their subjects intersect with food systems issues. Those student articles may also be distributed through other university news publications if they intersect with those specific topics, such as the Great Lakes Echo, if relevant to the Great Lakes region, or the Capital News Service, if relevant to Michigan Public Policy.
Notes: Eric Freedman wrote to me on Jan. 21: “Food Fix is currently dormant – no podcaster interest and no funding.”

Structure:
The Great Lakes Echo is a regional environmental news service run by the Knight Center for Environmental Journalism at Michigan State University. An adjunct professor edits the stories, most of which are produced by MSU students. Content is free to use by news outlets in the region at no fee or charge. The Echo covers news on and for the Great Lakes states, particularly the parts of the states that are in the Great Lakes Basin.

Distribution: Stories get picked up by other news media, environmental groups, government agencies, and industry groups. Michigan-centered news is also re-disseminated through MSU’s other community news program, the Capital News Service.

The Michigan State University Capital News Service is in its 44th year of covering statehouse news, with an average of five to six students, graduate and undergraduate, per semester in the spring and fall. The four-credit class is capped at 15 students. While students are not required to be at the Capitol every week, the class includes off-campus interviews most Monday mornings with influential players such as the governor, a state agency head, an advocacy group leader or a legislator to kickstart stories that are due at the end of each week. The legislature meets year-round with some breaks and is three miles from campus. Former Michigan Capital reporter Eric Freedman instructs the course.

Distribution: Students provide news coverage to more than 35 news outlets.

A summer special topics course where journalism students go to Bonaroo Music & Arts Festival in Manchester TN. Media arts and live audio students are involved in TV production, while the faculty champion takes journalism students to cover the festival in partnership with the Tennessee Press Association. Stories are written in real-time and sent out to every newspaper in Tennessee to use for free.

The program is a non-academic unit under the university's Scripps College of Communication where students volunteer their time to gain experiental learning in the program's newsrooms. Students produce 30 minute newscasts weekly, provide coverage on high school and university sports, host a sports talk show on AM radio and on podcast, and a news information podcast. Some students (mainly department heads) have received pay. Students work for $15 for 15 hours a week, a rate set by the university.
The program currently employs 6 professional staff who supervise/edit student work across each area.
The program has some informal relations between external programs, but does not consistently publish work outside its platforms. Students produce a nightly TV newscast that airs Monday-Friday at 6:30 pm.which includes news, weather, and sports. Students also produce a handful of regular sports podcasts thorughout the year.

Since 2011, Ohio University’s Scripps College of Communication has placed one to three interns in a local news bureau to cover the Columbus statehouse for an entire semester. Students can work in print, broadcast or other media when on this beat, depending on what newsroom they intern with. OU’s campus in Athens is about an hour’s drive away from the state capitol in Columbus. Students typically live in the capital city during their internship, often staying with their family. Students are paid about $6,900 as a stipend for the semester and required to work as an intern covering the statehouse for 200 hours that semester. All journalism students at OU are required to take some sort of semester-long internship, though not all opt to cover the legislature. The student salaries are funded by the Scripps Howard Fund and fellows' host news organizations. Students can opt to get 1 to 3 credits during the semester, but unlike a full internship, it requires them to pay tuition. Professionals at the newsroom housing the interns assign and edit their stories while faculty member Dr. Thomas Suddes provides simultaneous mentoring. Suddes, who has 30 years of statehouse reporting experience, also has professional connections with the local journalism market that helps students secure internships. Mostly journalism students have this internship, but some political science students have also participated.

The program is a community journalism and citizen training program for students and community members. The program recruits students throughout Oklahoma as part of an independent study. These students typically write about their home communities. 
The training program is an entry-level general education program that enables high school juniors and seniors to take the course for free.
The program is for independent study/internship credit. The main editor is both champions, but the program is looking to hire a professional editor.

It’s worth clarifying: our college on University Park does not work with commonwealth campuses outside of rare occasions as they operate separately and typically don’t have journalism programs.

 Going forward, we’d appreciate you limit your requests for data to me. Requesting data separately from different faculty is confusing for us and we’re already overwhelmed with deliverables. For example, Centre Documenters coverage is distributed in various ways — via the national network database (which you could consider a partnered organization if you choose), via sign ups for the newsletter or the text message service receive in their inbox or cell phones or they can organically search as they choose. We have other methods we’re in R&D around that we’re not including in the metrics we shared with you, like church bulletin inserts. In a rulal community with a shrinking media landscape, we’re getting more and more creative by necessity with a focus on meeting people where they are

Santa Ana College’s student reporting program operates through el Don, a credit-based, faculty-edited newsroom embedded across multiple journalism and media studies courses, where students are required to serve as newsroom staff and publish content as a condition of receiving course credit. The core course, JAMS 123A (Multimedia Production), emphasizes cross-platform reporting, requiring students to produce and adapt stories for web, social media, and multimedia formats, while advanced courses (123B–D) increase publishing expectations and introduce student leadership roles such as Editor-in-Chief, Managing Editor, and Copy Editor. The program engages approximately 30–40 students annually and produces around 300 stories per year, all of which include a required photography component, reflecting a strong emphasis on multimedia storytelling. Students manage day-to-day newsroom operations, but all content is reviewed and edited by faculty prior to publication to ensure professional standards. The program also maintains an informal partnership with Voice of OC, which publishes a small number of student stories annually, serving as a limited external distribution channel. AI use is minimal and pedagogically framed through a “human sandwich” model, where it may assist with brainstorming or refinement but is not used to generate journalistic content, and a formal AI policy is currently under development. Overall, the program emphasizes local reporting, multimedia literacy, and hands-on, in-person journalism training within a structured, curriculum-integrated newsroom model.

The program is a co-curricular unit within the College of Human Development, Culture, and Media. Stories are published in the university's student media outlets and through the program's partners (Fox, NBC, ESPN, Hoops HQ). The faculty champion directs the program and edits all stories.

"Senior journalism capstone students work with
Investigate Midwest on public affairs journalism
projects that are eligible of publication at the site. Faculty teach the course and assist students."

The Arrow student news organization operates as a complex news-academic partnership between the university and Rust Communications, the parent company of the local newspaper
. The program is overseen by a leadership team composed of four professionals from the partner organization, the Arrow’s faculty advisor, and the department chairperson, who acts as the budget custodian
. While the partnership follows a publisher agreement model, editorial decisions are made entirely by a paid staff of 10 to 12 student workers
. Academically, the Arrow serves as an experiential learning mechanism embedded as a lab within various journalism courses; students in these classes receive assignments from the student editorial staff to fulfill course requirements, which are then edited by students for publication
. The university supports this structure by providing equipment, housing, and faculty oversight for administrative processes like training and employment, while maintaining the integrity of the Arrow as a student-run publication

The media programs at the university fall under student affairs. The faculty champion is working to integrate their communications department and student affairs to work with students on submitting stories either to the student newspaper, The Lion's Roar, or to local media. The faculty champion also has a number of connections to local outlets and has helped students get their work published in the past.

This student club works with The Free Press, a completely digital platform for local and national news to publish students’ local news stories. Time spent editing or writing counts towards students' required journalism practicum. Stories produced by students are seen by the established viewers of the Free Press. While Dr. Garvey does officially run the club, students are expected to pitch their own ideas and work together to peer edit the pieces before publication. As students gain experience, they may hold leadership positions. Dr. Garvey is the director of the club and assists the students as needed. In this statewide collaborative, eight universities including four HBCUs are partnering together to provide community news through paid student reporting year-round: Louisiana State University and Agricultural & Mechanical College; Grambling University (HBCU), Dillard University (HBCU), Xavier University (HBCU), Southern University (HBCU), Nicholls State University, Northwestern State University and Southeastern University. Each school will have initially two to four students who will get paid $12 an hour for up to 10 hours of work a week during the fall and spring semesters. Additionally, each school will host one full-time summer internship where the student will be paid $12 an hour to write stories for their local community outlets. Reporting can include print, digital, broadcast and social media. To encourage more stories out of classrooms, students reporting for a class can earn a $100 stipend for any publishable story at any of these schools. Individual schools can decide whether students can also earn credit by participating this program.
This program has a news-academic partnership with the Chattanooga Times Free Press, publishing student-reported community news stories.

Faculty leaders incorporate a project every semester into classes that are shared on the CT Collaborative news site for news outlets in the state. 

"It's more about integrating a project or identifying students who are working on a statewide story and connecting them to the collaborative." - Jodie

Molly and Julia Rendelman co-direct the lab. They work with group of students who take on stories with the goal of getting it published in either the student paper, or other local news outlets. The lab also has a strong relationship with the student paper, the Daily Egyptian. Capital News Illinois is the main local news outlet partner. Lab is not course-based, but there are instances in which students from Molly's classes contribute stories.

Institution Name: Southern University and Agricultural & Mechanical College
Structure:
Louisiana State University, which is leading the collaborative, is working with seven other higher education institutions in Louisiana to work with faculty and news outlets to produce student-reported community news across the state. Those schools are: Grambling University (HBCU), Dillard University (HBCU), Xavier University (HBCU), Southern University (HBCU), Nicholls State University, Northwestern State University and Southeastern University. The collaborative will be headed by two faculty at LSU: student media advisor Jeff Gauger and professional-in-residence Lisa Page, who graduated from Dillard University before working at the Times-Picayune and Washington Post. 
Distribution: As of mid-February, Gauger sends out a weekly email to the network of participating faculty and editors. He is also forming a website for the collaboration. Each school will have initially two to four students who will get paid $12 an hour for up to 10 hours of work a week during the fall and spring semesters. Additionally, each school will host one full-time summer internship where the student will be paid $12 an hour to write stories for their local community outlets. Reporting can include print, digital, broadcast and social media. To encourage more stories out of classrooms, students reporting for a class can earn a $100 stipend for any publishable story at any of these schools. Program Name: Utah College Media Collaborative, Amplify Utah - audio practicum class is what Ryan actually teaches.

Structure: Students at St. Bonaventure University’s Jandoli School of Communication report for the online news site TAPinto Greater Olean as part of an experiential learning news coverage course.
Notes:
As you know, our operation differs from what most other colleges do. St. Bonaventure has purchased a TAPinto site for our students to get real journalism experience and fill the gap in local media coverage. TAPinto requires its franchises to post at least one original story per day, which is how we arrived at 300 figure. We probably missed a few days, but the TAPinto organization gives us some leeway because we are student-staffed.
The course is Journalists’ Workshop. Students take it for two semesters. I teach it with my wife and colleague Anne Lee. We had four students in the Spring 2026 class and five this fall. We also benefit from students who write for us to gain internship hours (Our school requires 400 internship hours) but are not enrolled in the class.
 We keep the site updated over the summer – usually with the help of interns. Anne and I are administrators of St. Bonaventure’s summer program at Oxford University, so we actually work on the site from the UK over our summers.

For more than 45 years SUNY New Paltz has been running the Legislative Gazette, an online media outlet that reports on the New York Legislature. (3-12 cr.) Supervised experiences in professional workplace settings for qualified upper-level Communication Studies, Broadcast Journalism and Media production majors.  Students combine knowledge, skills and theory learned in the classroom with practical application in a variety of private and public organizational environments (across in-person, remote and hybrid modalities). In the Journalism Program, community reporting is done in conjunction with our city's daily newspaper, the Press-Republican, in these classes:
JOU402, Newspaper Practicum, where occasional Plattsburgh community stories are shared with and published by the community daily. Offered every semester.
JOU403, Online Magazine Practicum, which was refocused on community news starting in the Spring 2026 semester, stories regularly published by the community daily. Offered every semester.
JOU308, News Writing and Reporting, Students write community news in direct collaboration with the community daily.Offered every 2 or 3 semesters.
JOU206, Introduction to News Writing, Where occasional Plattsburgh community stories are shared with and published by the community daily. Offered every 2 semesters.
In our other less frequent writing classes -- Travel Writing, Feature writing, Outdoor Writing, Sports Writing, for example -- instructors will identify occasional stories of community interest and bring them to the daily paper for possible publication.

In JOU411, Print Magazine Practicum, students write tourism magazine stories about our region in cooperation with the North Country Chamber of Commerce and publish these stories in our own glossy student-edited magazine, DoNOrth, which we distribute in the region.

Most of these efforts are longstanding, although the refocusing of JOU403 is new and this semester's JOU308 has a stronger emphasis on collaboration with the Press-Republican than in the past. These two efforts have been led by Stephen Kurczy.

Students volunteer and intern at NPR member station WAER (a Syracuse University licensee), working in areas including sports coverage, news reporting, podcast and audio editing, web writing, on-air hosting and social media content creation.

At the S.I. Newhouse School of Public Communications, student media production is anchored by Newshouse.com, a long-running, school-supported digital platform that functions both as a curricular publishing outlet and as an open contribution space for student reporters, editors, photographers, videographers, and data journalists. Content is produced through multiple classes as well as volunteer participation and is publicly distributed through Newshouse, with selective cross-publication through affiliated and external partners including WAER. In addition, the school operates a year-long, topic-driven reporting project open to students of any major, focused on issues of public relevance such as immigration, misinformation, or Title IX equity, with work published on a project-specific site and distributed through national and regional outlets including USA. Today, Syracuse.com, and Indian Country Today. Across platforms, at least 70 students contribute annually, producing approximately 250 stories per year, with 33 major multimedia packages generated by the signature project and 12 stories picked up by four external partners. The program positions student journalism explicitly as gap-filling community coverage while maintaining strong institutional control and professional standards. Unlike many student media environments, Newhouse actively integrates AI instruction into newsroom practice, encouraging transparent, productivity-oriented uses- such as data analysis, ideation, and interview preparation- while rejecting AI-generated content creation and working toward a formal, written AI policy.
Texan News Service stories are written by students in journalism and broadcasting classes and can be reprinted or reposted at no cost to partnering news outlets after they appear on

Class works with instructor to co-edit stories before submitting them to the papers they're assigned to for the semester. Institution Name: The University of Texas at El Paso
Structure: The program is both course-based and internship-based. It includes COMM 4313 (Senior Capstone), COMM 2344 (Digital Audio and Video Production), and COMM 3313 (News Gathering and Investigation), all of which provide hands-on journalism training. The University of Alabama owns three public-facing news outlets, which operate out of the university’s Digital Media Center: the television station WVUA 23, Alabama Public Radio, and the Center for Public Television. The media outlets each function as independent, standalone organizations, though they work in conjunction with the Department of Journalism and Creative Media, offering multiple opportunities for students to contribute to local news as interns or volunteers. Different opportunities may be paid or for course credit.  Through these opportunities, students participate in the newsroom, interacting with full-time professional journalistic staff, attending daily news meetings, and pitching and producing stories. Classes within the Department of Journalism and Creative Media may also interact with the news outlets, including by using lab space in the Digital Media Center. In one graduate-level class, students have opportunities to develop and pitch stories for Digital Media Center outlets. The Digital Media Center offers opportunities for students from disciplines across the university, not only students in the Department of Journalism and Creative Media.

The media outlets each function as independent, standalone organizations, though they work in conjunction with the Department of Journalism and Creative Media, offering multiple opportunities for students to contribute to local news as interns or volunteers. WVUA represents an atypical hybrid model in which a university-owned, commercial television station simultaneously serves the general public across seven counties in West Alabama and functions as an intensive student training environment. Unlike student publications oriented toward campus audiences, WVUA produces daily local newscasts for community viewers, sells advertising, and operates with a small professional staff overseeing a predominantly student workforce. Approximately 70–100 students participate annually across newsroom, sports, and production roles, contributing to an estimated 7,000 stories, with students responsible for nearly all reporting and much of the anchoring. Students must either receive academic credit or paid internship compensation, with strict volunteering largely eliminated, and typically progress from writing and editing roles into on-air reporting as they gain experience. Editorial review is conducted primarily by professional staff, though student producers may edit copy during evenings and weekends. The station covers a wide range of community issues—politics, courts, construction, public meetings, and events—though reporting tends toward event-driven coverage due to time and experience constraints. WVUA maintains a conservative stance on AI, prohibiting its use in content creation while allowing limited applications for transcription and audio cleanup, reflecting both industry norms in broadcast journalism and institutional caution.

The program exists under the science and environmental journalism major. Students in a course co-publish their stories with the Fairbanks Daily News-Miner. Students in the program are required to take an internship, in the past students have taken the internship with the public radio station as well. Stories are edited by the faculty champion.

Arizona Sonoran News currently is an upper-level class, and one of the options students have to do professional practice. Kendal teaches the course with an adjunct who is a full-time journalist. Students find stories, pitch them, reporting them and then produce them. A goal is to also translate as many stories as possible in Spanish. Professors edit and send weekly/bi-weekly email newsletters pitching stories to outlets. This version of AZSN started 3 years ago. In some limited cases, students from other classes have also contributed to the newswire.
Notes: Starting next year, Arizona Sonoran News will be a capstone for all students graduating from the program. Thi class only taught in Sprig 2025.

Structure: The program is an off-shoot of a cohort called Community Journalism. The faculty champion directs the cohort and teaches the newswriting class. The other part of the cohort is a class where students conduct photojournalism. The faculty champion is responsible for vetting, selecting, and sending the stories produced in the cohort to the media partner (KUAF, public radio station licensed by the University of Arkansas). Students receive credit for both courses.

Program Name: Advanced Radio Reporting/ KUAF Public Radio
Structure: The faculty champion is the senior producer for a local news magazine show for an NPR station in NW Arkansas. The faculty champion conducts reporting and producing and co-hosting for a show called Ozarks At Large. In the spring of 2023, the faculty champion started as an adjunct instructor at the University of Arkansas teaching a radio reporting class. The students work with the faculty champion, gaining experience in producing stories being in a newsroom. The course is for credit and stories are edited by the faculty champion.

The university launched a Community Reporting course in fall 2025 designed to function like a working newsroom; students will work with local media partners to report on community issues in the greater Buffalo region.

UC Berkeley Graduate School of Journalism has two community news sites, Richmond Confidential and Oakland North, both of which are housed within the School’s Investigative Reporting Program. These news sites have a total of five classes devoted to them, three for Oakland North and two for Richmond Confidential. About 60 graduate students write for these two sites in the fall via those classes. Come January, when those classes end, news reporting on those sites decreases. Spring semester offers a Local News Enterprise class, whereby about a dozen students work on longer form projects, about one story per month. These stories are not always publishable as they have to reach a certain bar. In the summer, the program has two paid interns, one per news site and who each publish stories at least twice weekly. 
Distribution: In addition to Berkeley’s two news sites, students also produce stories for local news organizations. The program works with two consistent daily news sites where student work gets published in: The Oaklandside and Richmondside, both community newsrooms launched by a Bay Area nonprofit called CitySide. The program is part of several external collaborative initiatives, including the California Local News Fellowship and the California Reporting Project, which tasked students to investigate police excessive force records and cases across the state in collaboration with local newsrooms. Major investigative projects like this are in outlets like the Dallas Morning News, LA Times, Grist, and the San Francisco Chronicle. The program also developed a project funded by the SCAN Foundation called “Aging in America.” Paid interns will report on aging issues, such as rural transportation. So far, 40 Aging in America stories have been published in the New York Times, local NPR stations, the Oregonian, and other outlets. While the grant funds only go so far (not every student gets to travel), they can fund student travel expenses. 
Looking forward: The program just launched a program called The Stakes, a UC Berkeley Journalism project looking at the Trump administration’s executive orders and its effect on California communities, including various cuts in federal funding and suspension of grants. Here’s an example.
Notable: What makes student publication at Berkeley unique is that students hold the copyrights to their stories, so Berkeley cannot offer them to other publications without the student’s permission.
Notes:
Hi Richard, 
I had a hard time filling out your survey because the way our students cover local news mostly is not through any partnership. UC Berkeley runs its own local news sites (Oakland North and Richmond Confidential). In 2025, those sites combined published 160 stories. But students have the option of pitching their stories elsewhere, through their own initiative -- usually without the school's involvement. In the 2025-26 school year, we had a special class devoted to the effects of new executive orders and policies on Californians, which we needed publishing partners for because those stories were not local to Richmond or Oakland. We published 20 stories in that project since the fall -- bringing the total to 180, if you want to count the statewide stories, which did appear in local outlets. 
I can tell you that 57 students wrote for our two websites and for the statewide project (or 48 wrote specifically for the two local websites and nine wrote for the statewide project). Stories from our statewide project were published in: CalMatters, San Francisco Business Times, San Francisco Chronicle, Fresno Bee, CapRadio, Contra Costa Pulse, San Francisco Patch, the San Jose Mercury News and LAist.
I can't tell you all the places where students published their own work, but they definitely published this school year on these local sites The Oaklandside, Richmondside Berkeleyside and the East Bay Times, as well as on KALX, KALW and KQED public media stations. (I think that's at least 17 publishing partners, though we don't have formal partnerships with any of the places I mentioned -- except for maybe the public media stations.)
Since our students own the rights to their stories — even those published on our two local news sites — they are free to form their own partnerships and write for whatever publications they choose. I would say at least a dozen students publish on their own each semester on sites I haven't mentioned here. We don't keep tabs on those. In addition, all of our students are required to do 300 internship hours, so they are producing stories for other publications through those internships. 
I hope this information is helpful, Richard. Sorry I did not respond sooner but the questions were daunting, given the explanation I just provided. 
Good luck. 
-Christine

Structure: It's a course-based reporting program. In classes, students' work is edited and shared with the Orlando Sentinel and the local PBS and NPR affiliates. Through class assignments, students cover community issues that help augment the local coverage of partner outlets.

The program will draw student-produced content from 3 different courses in the university: a capstone, Digital Media: Student Experience, an english department course, Journalism, and an interdisciplinary/internship course. The editor will depend on the course they're taking.

During the spring 2024 semester, about 10 to 15 students covered the Colorado Statehouse for the Colorado Student News Service serving 10 rural news outlets, providing them with print and multimedia content. Beth Potter, who has about 30 years of journalism experience including 10 years overseas, taught the three-credit elective course as an adjunct professor. The class met twice a week, and students traveled to the statehouse twice over the entire semester. Students traveled from campus to the statehouse via a 30-minute bus ride, which is free for students. They shared space on the press row with professionals. Colorado’s legislature only meets in the spring. During the following fall semester, Potter aimed to continue political and legislative coverage by holding the class and having students work together to report a ballot initiative for the 2024 election concerning hunting mountain lions. Not all students’ work produced during the two semesters in which the class operated was published by partners. During the spring semester, a few stories were published among the dozen or so students in the class. During the fall semester, one major story jointly reported by the class was distributed across the state. This year, she is pausing it indefinitely because she was promoted in her newsroom role outside of UC Boulder, along with other competing duties. Despite her pausing it, she said journalism students are still going to the Capitol voluntarily to pursue stories for student media, partly due to word of mouth on the success of the last two semesters.

Capstone class that is encouraged for and prefers seniors who are near the end of their college experience. Students develop in-depth stories over the semester and report on them, either individually or in teams. News partners are involved in pitch process, and are sent links afterward. In the past, has worked with Colorado Sun as well as smaller publications in Denver, such as the Bucket List. Regular partners also include Colorado Public Radio and Denver Gazette. "Strong narrative stories that focus on original reporting and explanatory reporting. Human-centered, human-focused. And then multimedia that really makes that pop." - Charles Plunkett

Program Name: Connecticut Student Journalism Collaborative, News Writing 2 class
Structure: Faculty teach a course that is the second in a two-part series of in-depth reporting classes, the second of which teaches students how to do community reporting and identify high quality works and offer to the regional collaboration. The University of Delaware in the spring of 2026 will launch a six-credit fellowship from January to June. Students will get one credit in January, three credits during the school semester, and two more credits for continuing into June. The fellowship will be offered for four to two students and is open to students majoring or minoring in public policy, political science, political communications, journalism, or other communications. The fellowship will fulfill a capstone degree requirement for any arts and sciences student participating at the 400 level. It also fulfills electives for the journalism minor. Nancy Karibjanian, Director of the Journalism Program at the University of Delaware who will be running the program, hopes to limit the fellowship to juniors to avoid making a senior commit to the June portion of the program. UD’s campus in Newark is about 50 minutes away by car from the state capitol in Dover. Every student will also be asked to follow one bill, with Karibjanian’s approval for newsworthiness. 
Distribution: The program will mix broadcast and digital print reporting. The outlet’s editor will edit the students’ work, and Karibjanian plans to use a Listserv to distribute stories to partnering organizations. Students can take assignments from local radio or news outlets at any point during the semester. Karibjanian will review any assignments from the outlets to approve the story concept for its legitimacy and relevance for a student journalist.
Looking forward: It’s unclear if transportation can be arranged or if students will have to drive themselves. Karibjanian said students may have to start by listening to the live stream of floor debates as a backup, in case they can’t transport themselves to the statehouse in person. 
Notable: Karibjanian wants the students to cover stories other outlets are not covering; she is still working out partnerships with local outlets beyond Delaware Public Media and is exploring partnerships with other local radio and broadcast stations.
A required capstone course where students make short-form documentaries and an 800-word digital story that are shared with Rocky Mountain PBS, which airs some of the final films.

The University of Florida’s Fresh Take Florida runs two semesters per year and employs up to 18 students per semester to cover the Florida Legislature, which is 2.5 hours from their campus in Gainesville. The three-credit class started in 2019 and operates during both the spring and fall semesters. While students are not usually paid, the program covers travel, dorm stays at Florida State University’s campus in Tallahassee and access to public records thanks in part to donations via the University of Florida Foundation. While many students will choose to take the class for two semesters, single-semester students will typically opt to take the class in the spring because it coincides with the legislative session. Most students come from within the journalism program. Students must write and publish five stories for the semester, and some have gone beyond that number. One of the five stories has to focus on an underrepresented community

Structure:
Noticias WUFT, a Spanish-language news operation housed within public media, is dedicated to informing, educating, and connecting the Hispanic community in North Central Florida through accurate, culturally relevant, and community-focused journalism. As part of this initiative at the University of Florida, students enrolled in journalism and communication courses produce content across television, radio, and digital platforms.
The program established a bilingual student newsroom that not only serves community information needs but also provides professional training, with many graduates advancing into major media markets. Operating under an immersion-for-academic-credit model within the UFCJC Division of Media Properties, student work is closely supervised and edited by faculty. This model has since been adopted more broadly across the College, enabling students to integrate experiential learning directly into their coursework.
Independent public radio station WUFT is staffed by student reporters, editors and producers.
