Structure: Grady Newsource is a broadcast and digital-first news service via a journalism capstone class serving five counties in Northeast Georgia, and has partnerships with local news outlets to pick up and republish stories.

The Oglethorpe Echo is a weekly newspaper currently operating as a nonprofit, and editorially staffed by student reporters in a capstone class managed by a full-time professional journalist who servces as the paper's editor.

For one weekend every spring, 18-20 photojournalism students descend on a rural county in northern Georgia, reporting stories about the communities and people who live there. First launched in 2006 by lecturer Mark Johnson, the workshop is the signature event of the Grady College of Journalism and Mass Communication’s documentary photojournalism course each spring. (Student enrolled in the fall semester get a similar experience during a workshop at the state fair.) Over the last 20 years, the workshop has been held in 17 different counties. Before the workshop begins, students develop and pitch story ideas, so when they arrive in the county for that weekend, they’re ready to jump into their reporting. Johnson invites experienced photojournalists to come work through the weekend as coaches, reviewing the material students gather with them each day. Before each workshop, Johnson establishes partnerships with local news outlets to distribute the stories. After each workshop, local news partners often publish initial photos spreads. Then, the outlets have the option to pick up students’ projects, including photo essays and multimedia pieces. The student work is also published on the college’s website.
 
The class includes a mixture of students in their second and third photojournalism semesters. This mixture creates a dynamic where the more experienced students serve as mentors for students who are not as far along. “Part of the program's core belief is that knowledge is not yours, and so you have a responsibility to help others within the class,” says Johnson. “The goal is for everybody to get better at this.”

The University of Hawaiʻi at Mānoaʻs journalism program began collaborating specifically on state legislative coverage in Spring 2022 and chose as its partner an innovative online-only news organization called Civil Beat. Students focus on reporting on the spring session of the Hawaii State Legislature in Honolulu. Each student selects a beat at the beginning of the semester and is responsible for coming up with story ideas connecting that beat with current legislative action. In the first year, the program had 12 students participate; in 2023, they had 15 students; in 2024, another 22 students were involved.
Student reported multi-media stories are shared from a capstone class  in partnership with local media as part of a new news bureau.

Program Name: McClure Journalism Internship Program/State Capitol News Bureau
Structure: The University of Idaho’s Journalism and Mass Media McClure Journalism Internship Program hosts about two students per spring semester to cover the Idaho Legislature in Boise, which is 300 miles away from the university campus in Moscow. During the week, students also get additional media training through the School’s news service, Idaho Public Radio. Students spend about every weekday and sometimes weekends, depending on whether there’s a news event, during January, February and March working at the statehouse during the session. Students tend to be juniors and 11 seniors. The program is open to any student; international studies majors participated in 2022 and 2023. The course is recognized as a 400-level internship and includes an average of 9 credits, though some students do less.

Since 1972, the University of Illinois Springfield has offered a Master’s Degree in Public Affairs Reporting. The 10-month program allows students to have hands-on experience with political reporting. The program is funded through the university, along with the help of private donors and alumni. With the funding structure of the program in addition to stipends and scholarships offered to students, Illinois residents are able to complete the program for around $6,000. The program is run by Jason Piscia, a longtime newspaper editor and reporter, and a graduate of the program. In a typical year, Piscia teaches and oversees around 10 students.

Stories are published on the Illinois Public Media sites, where their established viewership can read them. Most of the stories are pitched by students themselves and then edited by either Evans or one of the paid deputy producers – students who are more experienced and can help guide their peers. In 2020 the student newsroom produced more than 170 student pieces, and that number doubled the following year resulting in about 350 student stories in the 2021-22 school year. There are two classes of students that are involved with the program and three students are hired each semester to work as deputy producers. The program is funded by the University.

Students report stories for two community papers, The Mount Vernon-Lisbon Sun and the Solon Economist under the direction of editors and faculty in a partnership between the student paper, The Daily Iowan,  and the School of Journalism and Mass Communication.

Program Name: Good Morning Indian Country Radio News Station
Structure: Good Morning Indian Country is a student-led native news and information program. Students produce 30-minute shows that are formatted like a traditional local television broadcast. Students from the University of Kansas Journalism School collaborate with students from Haskell Indian Nations University. Recently found a partner in an local outlet that republished content

The three-credit capstone class started in 2012 and hosts between 10 and 18 journalism students, mostly seniors, every spring semester. Students typically go to the statehouse once a week during the session, which runs from January through May. They end their coverage when the semester ends, not when the session ends. Washington Post editor Patricia Weems Gaston and focuses largely on day-to-day coverage with stories available to about 190 news outlets in Kansas. Students commute 30 minutes by car to Topeka and can either be a reporter or an editor depending on their career ambitions.

Terri Finneman acts as the executive editor for an all-student staffed publication that is the paper for Eudora, Kansas. "We cover the towns, and we're covering city hall and school board, sports, business, features." - Finneman
Notes:
We were open 48 weeks last year and have 10 slots in our newsletter that is filled each week (and sometimes overfilled so not everything gets in), so that number is correct. These are part-time jobs where each student is producing 3 pieces of content a week on average. I explained on the call that "stories" is too strict of a word cuz our count also includes things every now and then like staff reports of dean lists and photo galleries and sports shows. But 480 pieces of content is correct. I also had a full-time post-grad reporter for 9 months of 2025, but my part-time staff kept up the workload the last 3 months of the year.
   Staff size differs throughout the year. I had 2 in the summer. 4-6 in the fall. It's hard for me to give specifics for these surveys because we are not like the others in your sample since we're a full-on outlet.

Structure: Students publish their work on an internal student-run news site designed for community-oriented journalism which shares the content with local news outlets.
Located on Capitol Hill, the CNS Washington bureau has more reporters dedicated to covering the federal government’s impact on Maryland than any other news organization in the state.

Program Name: The Howard Center for Investigative Journalism
Structure: This center, funded by the Scripps Howard Foundation, collaborates with professionals and students in Maryland and around the country — including other universities — to report investigative stories on topics such as sports, gambling and poverty.

Program Name: The Shirley Povich Center for Sports Journalism
Structure: The Shirley Povich Center for Sports Journalism prepares students to be innovators and leaders in all facets of sports media. The center’s unique, experiential curriculum and public programs elevate and amplify discussion of race, gender, politics and the world.

Program Name: Capital News Service Data & Graphics Bureau
Structure: Students in the Data & Graphics Bureau develop innovative data-driven web applications, interactive graphics and in-depth web projects. They serve as lead producers for CNS’ destination website and help test innovative new ideas to advance the practice of journalism.

CNS-TV consists of reporters who cover news from across the state and Washington, D.C.  They create video packages and live shots on deadline which air in a live, half-hour newscast that is produced by a team of producers and a production crew.  The program airs on UMTV, the University’s television station,  and is distributed by Comcast Cable to subscriber households in Prince George’s and Montgomery Counties. It also streams live on several social media sites.

The University of Maryland’s Capital News Service Annapolis Bureau is one of five news bureaus managed by The Philip Merrill College of Journalism. All journalism (M.J. not M.A.) graduate students are required to work in one of the news bureaus, whereas undergraduates have to apply for it as a capstone. (Graduate students apply for their preferred bureau, but do not always receive their top choice). Started in 1990, the bureau and the companion courses operate in the spring and fall. Graduate students get six credits for working four days per week. Undergraduate students get six credits for working two days a week or nine credits for working for days a week. The class size varies per semester, sometimes reaching as many as ten graduate and undergraduate students. The program is managed by long-time journalist and editor Martin Kaiser and Annapolis Bureau chief Christi Parsons. The statehouse is a 40 to 45-minute drive from campus.
In this program, mid-level undergraduate journalism students report news, often in their home communities in Maryland, for local news outlets.

Students in the collaborative have created content for the New Bedord Light, a digital nonprofit news outlet, as well as Cape and Islands NPR stations which cover Cape Cod, Nantucket, Martha’s Vineyard, the South Coast.

The free-standing Institute is housed in the Edward J. Meeman Journalism Building on the University of Memphis campus, though it is not formally part of the Department of Journalism and Strategic Media. It produces narrative, documentary journalism distributed through an exclusive contract with dailymemphian.com, a nonprofit, digital news outlet, as well as Memphis’ public radio affiliate, WKNO-FM. The Institute works with faculty and students within the Journalism Department as well as other departments of the University, including History, Sociology and Business.

The program is part of multiple classes, either where the program is part of the curriculum or where students have the opportunity to publish their work through the program. The program does not partner with co-publishers and is considered independent of the campus' student media. Editors consist of faculty who teach classes linked to the program.
Students work under the direction of the faculty leader with prominent local news outlet owner Forum Communications, providing news content for its 15 newsrooms in Minnesota.

The Mississippi Media Lab is currently structured as a student reporting program rather than a formalized news–academic partnership. A small team of students, primarily drawn from journalism courses, produces stories focused on community-centered issues, such as upcoming elections (e.g., voter registration and participation). Student work is developed under close faculty and staff supervision, with an emphasis on editing and professional guidance throughout the reporting process.
Notes: Marshall confirmed the structure.

Structure:
Students from three courses—TV Reporting (Fall), Advanced TV Reporting (Spring), and Digital Story Production (Fall)—produce either topic-driven or community-oriented stories as part of their coursework. These assignments focus on local issues, including coverage of the police department, school district policies, and broader community trends and events. As part of the course requirements, students create web versions of their multimedia or video projects. The faculty lead, Debora Wenger, oversees the process by supervising and editing student work to ensure professional quality. When a story meets publication standards, she forwards it to the local news outlet partner, The Oxford Eagle. Edited stories are first uploaded to a shared Google Drive folder, from which the outlet selects content for publication. This partnership was initiated and developed through the faculty member’s personal connections.
Distribution: The partnership primarily provides text and photos, even when the material originates from video-focused classes.
Notes: Deb Confirmed that prog

Structure:
Vox began in the spring of 1998 when the Columbia Missourian merged its two weeklies, Weekend, an entertainment magazine, and Ideas, the Sunday magazine. Since its inception, Vox has aimed to be a print version of that really cool friend of yours, the one who always knows what’s going on, can provide an intelligent viewpoint on any number of subjects, has a way with words that’s a combination between being snarky and knowing something, and is fun to hang with. Our lofty goals are outlined in our mission statement below.
The magazine is published by the Columbia Missourian and produced by magazine students at the world’s oldest (and we’d like to think best) journalism school. Don’t worry, it’s much more than some college newspaper. And we have faculty supervise us. Really. The magazine has won numerous awards, including the prestigious General Excellence award given by the Association for Education in Journalism and Mass Communications. In other words, Vox was named the best magazine produced by college students in the whole darn country. We also believe it is the only monthly magazine in the country produced by a staff that totally changes three times a year. Some think this is a challenge. We like to see it as a good thing — we never get stale or boring.
Students staff KBIA, the NPR affiliate and a public radio station licensed to that is operated by the journalism program the University of Missouri.

The University of Missouri’s State Government Reporting Program has had students working at the statehouse in Jefferson City for more than 40 years. About 20 to 30 students work in shifts to cover the legislative session from January to May under the direction of long-time government reporter and editor Fred Anklam. Because of the demand for radio stories, students can get credit or payment for their audio work, and most opt for credit. The stories for print or online publications also go through the copy desk of the Columbia Missourian, the daily newspaper that serves as the hands-on “lab” for journalism majors in their junior and senior years.
A statewide business news service staffed by students.

The program is course-based, offering Digital Storytelling, Advanced Multimedia Storytelling, and Broadcast Communication. These courses train students in multimedia and broadcast storytelling, but there is no mention of an internship requirement. There is no direct mention of a formal news-academic partnership, but the department is well-supported by faculty and administration. Given the success in student media competitions (BEA, SPJ, NATAS awards), students likely collaborate with external media, though formal partnerships are not explicitly stated. The students are emphasized to produce Multimedia storytelling, digital and broadcast communication; Developing high-quality media projects and Hands-on production skills in the media lab and studio. Multiple classes at the University feed the newsroom of a daily newspaper, the Columbia Missourian which is distributed across the county. Between 150-180 students serve as reporters, copy editors, designers, photographers and more under the direction of ten professors in the practice.
Students staff KOMU, the NBC affiliate and a commercial TV station with a broad reach, covering 38 counties in mid-Missouri via the broadcast.
Notes: Stephanie provided a new program Vox Magazine. Also some 0 means, we counted every program together. However, we recived separate info from Fred. Although Stephanie said that ner number includes Fred program as well. That's why I substraced numbers from the Missourian.

Structure: The program is primarily a course offered every fall at the university, available to journalism undergraduate and graduate students. Students are paired with a newsroom in Montana where they shadow professionals and do reporting typically over the course of 2 weekends. Students earn credits taking the course. Students covered stories based on the assignments of the editors of each news outlet. The faculty champion conducts a first-pass at editing, students also received editing support from the newsroom. Stories were published in print by 3 partner newspapers. The faculty champion also intends to partner with radio stations to publish stories.

The University of Montana Legislative News Service was started in 1993 in an attempt to fill the gaps in legislative coverage for the residents of Montana. The program is housed at the University of Montana and run by former legislative reporter Courtney Cowgill. Each legislative session, which happens every other year, two to three University of Montana journalism students move to the capital city of Helena to cover the session. While in the statehouse, the students are considered to be full members of the professional press corps covering the Legislature.

The Experience Lab is a curriculum-integrated experiential learning program that operates as a multi-platform, faculty-led newsroom model. Piloted in 2021 and fully implemented in 2022, it is a required one-credit, pass/no-pass course that students are required to take over three semesters. In each semester, more than 500 students take this lab. The program includes approximately eight student media labs spanning journalism, multimedia, PR, and sports, where students produce community-focused stories rather than campus news. Editorial oversight follows a layered structure, with student leads conducting initial edits, faculty providing final editorial control, and professionals-in-residence offering mentorship and feedback. Students produce a substantial volume of content—including hundreds of stories, newscasts, and multimedia outputs annually—which are primarily published on internal platforms such as the Nebraska News Service. That platform then functions “like a news wire service” and provides stories to 100+ community news organizations across the state. Led by co-directors Jill Martin and Kristian Anderson, the Experience Lab reflects a “teaching hospital” model that combines structured coursework with real-world reporting, professional mentorship, and community engagement.
Distribution: Text, video, photo.

Journalism and science students submit edited stories to public radio station KUNR, which edits and airs them. A Hitchcock student fellow supports the class but is also directly producing science stories with KUNR.
Students produce Spanish Language content that is aired on noticieromovil.com and made available to news partners.

University licensed public radio station KUNR supervises 3-5 interns a semester and airs content from the practicum class, the Lake Tahoe student staffed bureau and the Hitchcock Science Communications class as part of its local news coverage.
Three students in a required practicum course cover local stories in Lake Tahoe and aired on KUNR – a public radio station housed in the Reynolds School of Journalism
Planned to start in fall 2026 covering the elections  and the nex year the state legislature meets in its biennial session from January through May.
The program has built partnerships with multiple news outlets, and students have earned bylines in multiple publications.
Students in journalism classes produce content that is published on News Port and is distributed to partners.

The project is an online community newspaper which is part of the curriculum of the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill. Different classes make contributions to the program: for example, editing classes, business reporting classes, etc. The faculty champion also intends to open the program to community contributions. The main course that heads the program is called "Community Journalism." The program was founded earlier but was rebooted by the faculty champion 3 years ago. The program reports on major issues throughout the city and the faculty champion acts as the managing editor for student produced stories.
Student reporters cover community issues in a collaboration with the Cedar Falls News Hub.

For a semester, students move to Washington, D.C. and are paid $10 per hour, plus housing and transportation, to cover the U.S. Capitol and produce 20 to 30 stories each, edited by Gaylord College faculty and made available to 160 plus news outlets, including seven television stations and 10 Native American newspapers, across Oklahoma.

The University of Oklahoma’s Gaylord News covers the Congress year-round and the legislative session during the spring semester. There are two parts to this, a Washington, D.C. component in which students are paid $10 per hour, plus housing and transportation, and a 1-credit in-state practicum in the spring that covers the legislative session. Students based in Washington typically will write 20 to 30 stories each during the semester. Students working in the state capitol are expected to be at the statehouse at least one day per week when the legislature is in session, either for the entire morning or afternoon and produce at least 10 stories during the February to May session. Students commute from campus, which is 20 miles from the state capital in a suburb of Oklahoma City – about a 40-minute drive. As of February 2025, Washington and legislative content is provided free via listserv emails and Google Drive. Faculty are exploring ways to grow via a more intensive, higher-credit legislative class in the fall and spring semesters.
Catalyst Journalism Project has helped students publish longform investigative and solutions-focused journalism in local outlets since 2017.

Special topics course will partner with local outlets to increase news coverage throughout the state. Potential partners include Ocean State Stories, RI Suburban Newspapers, EcoRI, RI PBS/The Public's Radio and The Providence Eye.

The Community News Lab partners with media outlets to publish edited student content, for example in spring 2025, the CNL partnered with EcoRI, an environmental and social justice news outlet, to create aquaculture story maps using arcGIS.
Notes: Under one program name, two programs at the University of Rhode Island

Program Name: Civic Journalism and Social Justice Class
Structure: The class is one of several options within a program known as Richmond Endeavor, which the university uses to facilitate smaller cohorts within the 850-something first-year students who enter every year. Students sign up for one of the classes based on their interests, and classes can range from anything such as computer science and music. The class also covers the basics of journalism, why it’s important, and how to cover issues important to a community. Stories were short and included a church profile, a coffee shop profile, as well as reports on schools and neighborhoods.

Students in the Public Affairs Reporting and Advanced News Writing and Reporting classes at the University of Richmond cover the Virginia General Assembly and local issues as part of the Richmond Capital News Service, a bureau of Virginia Commonwealth University’s Capital News Service. Advanced News Writing and Reporting students take this course during the spring or fall and typically start getting assignments to cover local government issues midway through the semester. In the spring, some also join Public Affairs students and take on legislative assignments. Each Spring semester, 12 to 15 Public Affairs students provide daily coverage of the General Assembly for small news outlets in Virginia and distribute stories on the news service website and by direct request from publications.

The University of South Carolina's journalism Capstone program, known as senior semester, is divided into digital and broadcast sides. The digital side, comprising around a dozen students or more, contributes content to the school’s online publication, the Carolina News & Reporter. Digital students produce news articles, videos and visuals. The broadcast side partners with about 30 students. The program allows students to pitch stories, with up to half of multimedia students covering at least one story on politics, including the South Carolina statehouse.
Students in Broadcast News, Writing for Radio & Television, and Media Management produce stories for local news outlets under the direction of the faculty leader.

Program Name: Social Media Storytelling for Latino Audiences, Bridging the Gap Between LA Influencers and Independent Journalists, Spanish Language News Production.
Structure: University of Michigan-Flint does not have a journalism program, but Atkinson teaches two courses: news writing and news editing. Currently, students from Atkitson's classes write stories, and if they're of high-quality, Atkinson encourages them to submit the work to the campus student media, which was recently reinstated after being down for a year.

Journalism 499 is co-developed with a newsroom (L.A. TACO) and embedded into a university (USC) course where students will cover LA community issues and publish in the LA Taco. Because this is a university-led student reporting program, where faculty/staff (I believe it is you) supervise and edit student's contents and will be distributed to the LA community through LA TACO–USC .

Crosstown is a news website using public data collected by municipal entities/government agencies around Los Angeles and analyzed by computer science students at USC. Two to three students participate in the partnership per semester and produce about three stories at a time, along with a weekly newsletter tailored to individual neighborhoods covered. Students work with an editor hired for Crosstown.

Student reporting is published through the program's Public Understanding through Local and Statehouse Engagement (PULSE) project. The program also partners with the university's NPR program, WUTC, to provide them with local content. Students enrolled in the program are part of a course.

Students working at WUOT gain hands-on, professional experience in a real-world public radio newsroom, contributing meaningfully to reporting, production, and on-air content. As part of the NPR affiliate housed within the College of Communication and Information at the University of Tennessee, Knoxville, students collaborate with experienced journalists and broadcasters while developing skills in audio storytelling, news writing, interviewing, and editing. They are not only learning in a classroom setting but actively participating in the creation of content that serves East Tennessee audiences, preparing them for careers in public media and beyond.

The program partners with Univision (KUVN, Channel 23) Telemundo 39, NBC 5, and intends to partner with CBS 11. Production is exclusively TV news. Students are enrolled in the program unpaid, some examples of work include shadowing professionals/mentorships and producing content. Because Telemundo is a national news network, students have the opportunity to be published nationally. Stories are published in the Arlington Sentinal.

Students enroll in a 1-credit course taken up to 3 times, then students can take a 3-credit course if needed. The course consists of a mix of under/middle/upper classmen. The main editor is another CCN champion for the Arlington Sentinal.
Students produce stories for univesity run website that are shared with area media partners.

Students are matched with a news outlet, preferably from their hometown, but in reality, the value added is being in Austin because of the shrinking capitol bureaus. The students worked with their newsroom editor under the guidance of former New York Times editor Kathleen McElroy and former Dallas Morning News Capitol bureau chief Christy Hoppe.
Thomas has a relationship with a local news outlet and pitched the course, but his circumstances changed, and he is now at OU.

Program Name: Utah College Media Collaborative, Amplify Utah
Structure: Courses integrate real-world journalism projects in collaboration with professional media outlets. They also have strong partnerships with Amplify Utah, The Salt Lake Tribune, KRCL, The Other Side Academy, PBS Utah, and The Great Salt Lake Collaborative. Their content focuses on Community storytelling, underrepresented voices, homelessness, media literacy, and local news coverage.
CNS partners with 15 community news and statewide media, providing professionally edited student-reported stories to media partners.

Program Name: Media responsibility in a diverse society
Structure: Faculty leader partners with some ethnic, niche media in Seattle and throughout northwest. Students write one story each for those publications over the course of the 10-week quarter. Students come up with story ideas, pitch, report, photograph and whatever else is needed for story. Then story has potential of getting picked up by the publication.
Notes: We need to double check with her because I think she has a statehouse program. Maybe we are already aware.

Structure: The Department of Communication's Journalism and Public Interest Communication program every winter quarter pairs students with local and state outlets to cover news from the Capitol in Olympia during the legislative session. Starting in 2025, the department awards subsidies through a short application process to select small-budget independent and nonprofit news outlets seeking to participate in the program. Faculty member John Tomasic runs the program. The University of Washington is housed in Seattle, about an hour and 15 minutes away from the capitol by car. Students in this program are housed together via a short-term rental near the capitol instead of commuting. The housing plan is paid for by a donation from alums, totaling about $5,000 a year, that the program gives as stipends to the students who use it to pay rent in Olympia during the ten-week winter quarter when the legislature every year is in session. Additionally, the students get paid by the outlets for which they’re interning – about $3,000 per outlet for ten weeks. The department now occasionally provides grants to hard-pressed non-profit and independent outlets to pay for an intern.

The 20-year-old publication is an established, award-winning local news outlet that trains students and works with community members under the direction of the faculty advisor. While it has partnered with local news organizations in the past, the site currently collaborates on storytelling with nonprofits to produce solutions journalism along with other genres of content like memoir and op-eds. In addition to this, Madison Commons’ team of interns publishes in-depth and hyperlocal material in more traditional text and multimedia formats.

Program Name: Media Writing class, Harvey Hall News Service (unsure of what harvey hall news service is, I might have missed something)
Structure: Program is coordinated through a Media Writing course which uses different grants to fund opportunities for students to do community journalism and the instructor helps students with the stories and editing and publishing to a wordpress site that is shared.

A statehouse reporting class where students pitch stories and the instructor acts as the news director. Each legislator is assigned only one student to avoid overwhelming the legislator. Students do the first round of edits on each other’s stories before they are vetted either by Cox or a professional partner, pre-publication.

The Pressroom (JCOM 4530 - 004 THE PRESSROOM) is a new program at Utah State University as of the spring 2026 semester focusing on providing community coverage. The course description is as follows: "Calling all content creators — this course is for you and creating a professional portfolio. As a member of the Pressroom, you will publish articles, radio stories, podcasts, tv stories, sports with professional news outlets. You will also have professional opportunities to provide press releases, social media and communication support for local businesses (calling all PR students). You decide what you need for your portfolio and in this class, we build it together. This is a student-led pressroom, meaning you will have opportunities for leadership, production and management positions. You will join a network of more than 200 universities with student-led news, impacting communities directly across the United States."

Program Name: Utah College Media Collaborative, Amplify Utah
Structure: Amplify Utah creates a bridge for student journalists to publish with The Salt Lake Tribune and other local media and elevate diverse perspectives while providing emerging storytellers professional exposure.

Communciations program that covers 14 small towns around VSU-Lyndon and sometimes co-publishes footage with regional partners, esp TV stations in Burlington; students take for course credit in comms dept

The program houses graphic design, journalism, and TV production, and is under the journalism program (The Student Newsroom) where students produce content for the local radio station. Stories produced in the newsroom are also informally shared with the local newspaper. Stories are edited by a professional journalist who previously worked for the local newspaper (Sisko Stargazer). Students enroll in the program through a paid internship course and receive credit.

Students are matched with a local news partner, and they spend a semester (16 weeks) covering the state legislature and publishing regularly in the partner news outlet. The program pays a mentor in Olympia to work part-time during the legislative session with the students on story ideas and content before it is sent to publications for final edits. The news outlets pay the students for their work, and students can also receive academic credit.

Program Name: Utah College Media Collaborative, Amplify Utah
Structure: Amplify Utah creates a bridge for student journalists to publish with The Salt Lake Tribune and other local media and elevate diverse perspectives while providing emerging storytellers professional exposure.
A field course where students report on local communities and content is made directly available to community partners.
Students produce six thirty minute news shows each semester in a broadcast capstone class that are aired on a local TV channel.
The school offers the course every OTHER spring. (even years). The school is still involved in the WAMC collab.

KMUW operates two main internship programs. The first is the KMUW News Lab, developed in partnership with Wichita State University’s Elliott School of Communication. Each semester the station hosts two journalism students who work about ten hours per week in the newsroom, accompanying reporters to assignments such as city council meetings or press conferences while also producing short broadcast scripts (“readers”) and occasionally recording interviews for on-air stories. Interns receive professional training in broadcast writing and voice delivery, and their work is closely edited by reporters and the news director. The station also previously hosted marketing and engagement interns when staffing allowed. The second program is the Corva Coleman Diversity in Journalism Internship, a 10-week summer program created to increase representation of people of color in public radio. It is a fully immersive experience requiring 40 hours per week and provides a $5,000 stipend plus housing and meals. Applicants are recruited nationwide and mentored by NPR anchor Corva Coleman. KMUW serves roughly 720,000 people across 28 counties in Kansas, reaching about 70,000 listeners monthly. The station lost around 7% of its budget in federal funding, but donations from listeners—sometimes described internally as “rage giving”—have temporarily compensated for the loss. Cost-saving measures include freezing travel, leaving several positions unfilled, and relying on financial support from Wichita State University to partially fund the News Lab internships.

Program Name: WSU x Worcester Guardian Local News Partnership
Structure:
WSU partners with The Worcester Guardian, a free nonprofit news organization. Student journalists support the online publication as interns reporting stories for government, education, health, business, and culture beats. Students also contribute multimedia journalism, photojournalism, and social media management.
Distribution: Online

In this statewide collaborative, eight universities including four HBCUs are partnering together to provide community news through paid student reporting year-round: Louisiana State University and Agricultural & Mechanical College; Grambling University (HBCU), Dillard University (HBCU), Xavier University (HBCU), Southern University (HBCU), Nicholls State University, Northwestern State University and Southeastern University. Each school will have initially two to four students who will get paid $12 an hour for up to 10 hours of work a week during the fall and spring semesters. Additionally, each school will host one full-time summer internship where the student will be paid $12 an hour to write stories for their local community outlets. Reporting can include print, digital, broadcast and social media. To encourage more stories out of classrooms, students reporting for a class can earn a $100 stipend for any publishable story at any of these schools. Individual schools can decide whether students can also earn credit by participating this program.

Structure: Students in a Magazine Editing and Production class work with several local community news partners to publish the students work.
